title: The CISO Report 2026
gist: A survey of 650 CISOs by Oxford Economics and Splunk finds the security-chief role expanding rapidly to cover AI governance, secure software development, and personal legal liability, while defenders face AI-native threats like deepfake phishing and self-mutating malware and shift spend toward talent over technology.

themes.theme: CISO role expansion and personal liability

insights.claim: The Chief Information Security Officer role is expanding faster than most leaders can keep pace with, now spanning AI governance, secure software development, and mounting personal liability for security incidents.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: Rising personal liability for CISOs raises demand for D&O-style protection, security tooling, and governance platforms; the widening mandate signals sustained security budget growth.
insights.quote: Findings reveal a role expanding faster than most can keep pace with, as leaders take on AI governance, secure software development, and mounting personal liability for security incidents.
insights.locator: Findings reveal a role expanding faster

insights.claim: CISOs are forging joint accountability with C-suite peers and working to translate technical risk into business language boards can act on, reframing security from a cost center into a driver of business resilience.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: Security reframed as a resilience driver rather than a cost center supports premium pricing and board-level budget defensibility for security vendors and services.
insights.quote: turn security from a cost center into a genuine driver of business resilience.
insights.locator: This report invites the reader to uncover

themes.theme: AI-era threat landscape and defense posture

insights.claim: Enterprise security threats are growing more sophisticated with AI-generated deepfake phishing and self-mutating malware cited as emerging attack vectors.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: AI-native attacks favor vendors selling AI-driven detection and identity verification; watch for accelerating spend on anti-deepfake and behavioral-detection tooling.
insights.quote: from AI-generated deepfake phishing to self-mutating malware
insights.locator: Yet even as threats grow more sophisticated

insights.claim: Security leaders are prioritizing investment in talent over technology as their primary response to escalating threats.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: A talent-first spending posture tempers the bull case for pure-play security software and points to demand for managed services, training, and staffing.
insights.quote: They're investing in talent over technology
insights.locator: Yet even as threats grow more sophisticated

insights.claim: CISOs are navigating an AI paradox in which AI delivers productivity gains alongside significant implementation challenges and new risk-management burdens.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: The gap between AI productivity promise and implementation friction signals a durable market for AI-governance and secure-deployment tooling.
insights.quote: how CISOs are navigating the AI paradox, from productivity gains to implementation challenges and risk management
insights.locator: This report invites the reader to uncover

insights.claim: Oxford Economics partnered with Splunk to survey 650 Chief Information Security Officers between July and August of 2025 for the second edition of this report.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: A Splunk-sponsored study frames the demand narrative for security analytics platforms; treat conclusions as directionally useful but vendor-influenced.
insights.quote: Oxford Economics once again partnered with Splunk to survey 650 Chief Information Security Officers (CISOs), between July and August of 2025
insights.locator: In the second edition of the CISO report
